metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = negative contribution (downward, good for ground state), CRIMSON = positive contribution (would push energy up — but none exist for ground state)
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no derivation of the second-order formula, no sum-over-states computation, no comparison to variational upper bound, no discussion of excited-state behavior

beats.narration_text: A physicist adds an electric field to hydrogen. First-order correction: zero, by symmetry. Second-order correction: comes out negative.
She tries a quartic bump. Negative. A random Hamiltonian she invented on the spot. Still negative. Every time, it pushes the ground state down.
Perturbations point in all directions. Some should push energy up, some down. Yet every perturbation's second-order correction to the ground state is negative. Why?

props.headline: Perturbations point in all directions. Some should push energy up, some…

card.headline: Any perturbation, any direction — always negative.
card.dek: Why does every second-order ground-state correction push energy down?

beats.narration_text: The second-order correction is a sum over all other energy levels. Each term has a matrix element in the numerator and an energy difference in the denominator.
The numerator is a squared magnitude — a number times its own conjugate. It is never negative. That part alone cannot determine the sign.
So the sign must come from the denominator: the ground-state energy minus the energy of each excited level. That difference is always negative — every excited level sits above the ground state.

props.headline: So the sign must come from the denominator: the ground-state energy…

beats.narration_text: Here is every energy level in a quantum system. The ground state is at the bottom. Every other level sits above it. There are no levels below the ground state.

Each term in the sum is a negative number — the squared matrix element divided by E₀ minus Eₙ. Negative divided by negative is positive... wait. No: squared magnitude divided by a negative denominator is negative.
Every single term in the sum is a positive numerator over a negative denominator. The sum of all those terms is always negative — regardless of what the perturbation is.
The perturbation's form determines how big each term is — but not its sign. Electric field, quartic bump, or pure noise: every contribution still pulls down.

props.headline: The perturbation's form determines how big each term is — but…

card.headline: Numerator: |matrix element|^2 >= 0
card.dek: Denominator: E_0 - E_n < 0 always. Every term is negative.

beats.narration_text: This is not just a curiosity. It is why the variational principle works: any trial state gives an energy above the ground state, and the second-order correction approaches it from above.
Add any perturbation to any atom, molecule, or oscillator — its ground-state energy can only go down at second order. The ground state is a strict floor that perturbations erode, never build.

Illustrative: a particle-in-a-box with a small linear tilt. First three second-order terms: minus 0.031, minus 0.0014, minus 0.00018 — all in units of epsilon squared. She tries a quadratic tilt. Same story: every term negative, total minus 0.034 epsilon squared.
Any perturbation pushes the ground state down at second order — because the ground state has no levels below it. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: Any perturbation pushes the ground state down at second order —…

card.headline: No levels below — every term pulls down.
card.dek: The second-order ground-state correction is always negative.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Approximation Methods and Real Systems series.

props.seriesTitle: Quantum Mechanics: Approximation Methods and Real Systems
